macro_segments.title: Multiple Dates as Kiddushin – Collective vs. Individual Acts

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: Counting Out Dates and the Requirement of Shaveh Perutah
The Digression: If Kiddushin Fails, Is the Money a Gift or a Pikadon (Entrusted Object)?
Rav vs. Shmuel: Does the Woman Keep the Money If Kiddushin Fails?
The Challah Analogy: When Must an Invalid Transaction Be Returned?
Shmuel's Position: Returning Flour to Avoid Owner Confusion About the Law
Alternative Solution: Let the Kohen Keep the Flour but Tell Owner to Separate Again
Why This Alternative Fails: The Owner Won't Understand the Instruction
The Owner Doesn't Know the Reason – Only the Custom: Distinguishing Knowledge from Reasoning
Hydroponic Plants Case: Two Different Types (Trei Minei) Allow Second Separation
The Gemara's Challenge: Why Don't People Listen to Separate Again Even When It's the Same Object?
The Bitter Gourd / Sour Watermelon Precedent: People Do Listen to Separate Again
The Distinction: Even if People Don't Listen, the First Terumah Is Biblically Valid
Source from Leviticus: How Bad-on-Good Terumah Still Takes Effect

macro_segments.title: Return to the Mishnah: Rava's Explanation of Collective vs. Separate Acts with Eating

micro_segments.title: When the Mishnah Distinguishes: Saying 'With This, With This, With This' (Bezo Vezo Vezo)
If She Eats Each Date: Only the Last One (When Statement Finishes) Counts as Kiddushin
When He Says 'Marry Me with These' (Collective): All Dates Are Hers Once Named, Eating Permitted
Baraita Support: Acorn, Pomegranate, Nut – Interpretation as Collective (Ba'elu)
Gemara's Question: How Can Pomegranate Case Be Ba'elu If Language Suggests Individual?
Resolution: The Baraita's First Case (Ba'elu) Doesn't Distinguish Eating from Not Eating
Rav Ami vs. Rav u'Shmuel: Where Does the Eating Clause Apply?
Reconciliation: The Baraita Follows Rabbi (Rebbi), Not Rav u'Shmuel's Paradigm

macro_segments.title: Kiddushin with a Debt (Mekadesh B'Milveh) – Can You Marry with Money You've Lent Her?

micro_segments.title: The Basic Question: If She Owes You Money, Can You Marry Her by Forgiving the Debt?
Rav's Position: Milveh L'Hotza'ah Nitnah – A Loan Is Not Yours Once Given
The Money/Object Distinction: Loaned Money Is Immediately Not Mine; a Loaned Object Stays Mine
Apparent Contradiction: Mishna in Mechirah (Land Purchase) Allows Kiddushin B'Milveh
Rav Nachman's Resolution: The Case Is When the Amount Falls Short (Maneh Chaser)
Debate Within the Short-Payment Case: Is She Embarrassed to Demand the Remainder?
The Principle: Only If She Truly Accepts Receiving Less (Gmar Da'at) Does It Work
Threshold Cases: Much Less vs. Nearly Complete – When Embarrassment Ceases
Rav Elazar's Case: Marriage with 100 Promised but Only 5 Given (Down Payment)
Baraita on Pikadon (Entrusted Object): Does Marriage Work If Object Is Lost?
Baraita Positions: Whether Milveh Works Without Any Money Left vs. Requiring Remainder
Rava's Critique: The Baraita Text Is Corrupt; Reconciling It with Rav's View
Rava's Restatement of the Baraita: By Milveh, Even Some Remaining Money Doesn't Work
Rabbi Shimon b. Elazar's Counter-Opinion: Milveh Is Like Pikadon (Some Money Left Must Work)

macro_segments.title: The Debate on When a Loan Becomes the Borrower's Property: Possession vs. Spending

micro_segments.title: Two Views on Loan Possession: Immediate (Rav) vs. Only After Spending Begins (Shmuel)
Application to the Baraita: If Lender Retains Ownership Until Spending, He Can Still Marry with It
The Rav's Rabbis' Explanation: Debate Is About Retraction Rights (Chazarah), Not Liability
Comparison to Sheol (Borrowing Objects): Even Objects Accepted Immediately Incur Liability
Refined Position: All Agree on Immediate Liability (Achrayus); Debate Only on Whether Money Remains Lender's Property
Core Principle: When Does Abstract Debt Replace Physical Ownership?

topical_tags.term: Klali vs. Prati (collective vs. individual acts)
Milveh l'hotza'ah nitnah (loan immediately not owner's)
Money vs. object distinction in loans
Milveh bireshut be'alim l'chazarah (loan in original owner's possession for retraction)
